narrative: Dear [Student's Name], I know this rejection letter stings, especially after all the hard work you've put into your undergrad years and LSAT prep, but let me walk you through the story the admissions model saw in your profile—a tale shaped by patterns from thousands of students who've walked this path before. At the heart of it, your Year 3 decile of 9.0 stood out as the biggest signal pulling toward a tougher road to the bar exam, suggesting that even with solid performance, something in that later stretch didn't quite align with the trajectories of those who passed. Right behind it, your Year 1 decile of 9.0 echoed that concern, painting a picture of consistent high-decile standing that, in the model's eyes, surprisingly leaned toward struggle rather than strength for the bar. Your upper-middle family income added another layer, as it often correlates with profiles that faced hurdles despite resources. Then there's your LSAT score of 40.0 and undergrad GPA of 3.3—respectable marks that still nudged the prediction toward failure when stacked against top passers. Your background as a white male and full-time student mostly followed those trends too, though your gender actually tipped slightly in a positive direction, like a small vote of confidence. Together, these pieces wove a narrative where the odds of bar failure hit about 60%, enough for the school to pause on your application. It's not a judgment on your potential or drive—many with similar starts have defied the odds through targeted prep. Think of this as a nudge to dig into what held back those deciles or to boost that LSAT edge. You've got a 3.3 UGPA and strong deciles as a foundation; with focus, you could rewrite this story. Keep pushing—you're closer than it feels.
